data.title: Palantir: Not All AI Tools Are Created Equal
data.description:
PLTR stock: FQ1 and AI initiatives

I last covered Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) in late April. That article was entitled “Palantir Q1 Preview: Solid Rule Of 40 Score May Continue.” The article provided a preview for its Q1 earnings report (ER). It rated the stock as hold as “I expect its solid rule of 40 score to continue in Q1, a crucial factor for sustaining its high valuation ratios.”

Since then, there have been several new catalysts evolving around this battle-front stock (apologies for the pun). In this follow-up article, I will examine the top 2 catalysts on my radar. The first one is specific to PLTR’s operations. I will review the business updates it reported in the Q1 ER, especially in terms of its AI offerings. The second one is more general and involves the developments of the DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) since my last writing. After examining these developments, I keep seeing PLTR as a market leader – or even a market definer—for both commercial and government organizations to leverage AI products. Hence, this article reiterates my hold rating despite the extreme valuation multiples (with an FWD P/E of over 200x as of this writing).

Let me start with the business updates provided in its Q1 ER. As seen, the company continued to deliver growth surpassing consensus estimates (which are already aggressive to start with in my view). In the ER, PLTR provides updates on its AI offerings and product developments. My overall assessment from these updates can be summarized into these 7 words: not all AI products are created equal, and I will elaborate further next.

PLTR stock: not all AI tools are created equal

Under the AI fanfare, many companies are announcing AI initiatives. However, the AI tools that many of them rely on are often ChatGPT. I do see some value in ChatGPT’s general AI tools. However, I believe Palantir’s tools offer truly differentiated value for its customers based on the updates provided in the ER. The ER has provided numerous examples to showcase how PLTR’s tools are outcome-focused and can drive tangible results for its customers. For example, with PLTR tools, Heineken is transforming its supply chain using AI agents and their team built what used to take 3 years before in 3 months.

I expect the differentiated tools to further augment the speed and scale at which PLTR can deploy its products and convert customers going forward. The results provided in Q1 seem to support this expectation. As you can see from the ER slide below, Q1 saw 71% Y/Y growth and 19% Q/Q growth in U.S. commercial revenue growth Customer counts growth at very similar paces. Finally, note the 2x Y/Y growth of deals closed worth $1 M or more. These robust growth rates clearly reflect the effectiveness of both PLTR’s AI technologies and also its marketing approaches, in my view.

Before leaving this section, let me point out another timely tool that PLTR has in its pipeline. PLTR also rolled out a tariff management application recently. Given the uncertainties on this front since “Liberation Day,” I anticipate new products like this to keep attracting new customers to PLTR.

PLTR stock: my previous DOGE concern

Now, I will switch to the second catalyst – the changes in the broader geopolitical landscape. As mentioned in my last article, one concern I have over PTLR is its heavy reliance on U.S. government contracts. With efforts the DOGE was making at that time to trim government spending, I felt this risk was heightened at that time. However, the developments of the DOGE in the past 2 months or so have encouraged me to reassess this risk and I believe it is much lower than what I previously feared.

In the meantime, other developments have led me to see Palantir well positioned to benefit from the policy changes under the Trump administration. For example, recently, President Trump signed an executive order to modernize defense acquisitions. This EO made it clear the Department of Defense, a key contractor for PLTR, will pursue commercial-like contract terms and apply a “first preference for commercial solutions” going forward. I expect this EO to change the approach of many other defense contractors in their bidding of defense contracts, thus opening up new opportunities for PLTR given its unique strengths. Besides its leading and differentiated AI tools as aforementioned, another key strength for PLTR in my view is its integrated software and hardware efforts. Its TITAN program (see details from its investor presentation below) is a good example of this integrated approach.

Risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, Palantir's high valuation remains a cause of concern in my model. It’s current P/E of 206x as you can see from the following table, borderlines absurd levels in absolute terms. Even when compared to other high-growth AI companies, this is extreme and stands out as an outlier. Also, I mentioned that my previous analysis seemed to have overestimated the impacts of DOGE. But in the overall scheme of things, PLTR’s current revenue mix is still heavily relying on a relatively small number of clients, especially government customers. For example, according to this USB analysis, about 55% of Palantir's current revenue is derived from contracts with the U.S. federal government.

With such concentration, PLTR could be particularly sensitive to changes in its competitors’ offerings and technological advancements. And PLTR does face intense competition from both established tech giants and emerging startups, both for commercial and government contracts. The list of competitors ranges from familiar tech names such as Microsoft (MSFT) (in particular its Azure’s Government-specific offerings) and Amazon Web Services (AMZN) to more recent entrants such as Anthropic (whose "Claude Gov" models are directly targeting government AI contracts). Defense contractors such as Lockheed Martin (LMT), Boeing (BA), and RTX Corporation (RTX) have traditionally focused on hardware developments. However, these large defense contractors are now increasingly investing in advanced software, AI, and data analytics in their offerings for government projects. As such, PLTR must constantly invest heavily in research and development, and it remains to be seen if it can hold its competitive moat in the longer term.

To conclude, this follow-up article on PLTR has been triggered by 2 catalysts since my last writing: the business updates reported in the Q1 ER and the developments of the DOGE. These developments have led me to continue viewing PLTR as a leader – or even definer – in AI products. In particular, I expect the company to enjoy new opportunities with defense contracts given the anticipated change in the bidding approach, its differentiated AI tools, and also its integration of software and hardware. Based on these considerations, I maintain a hold rating despite the extreme P/E ratios.

data.title: Victoria's Secret Belongs Behind Closed Doors
data.description:
By Breakingviews

Victoria’s Secret (VSCO) is struggling with the public glare. Since becoming independent in 2021, the lingerie retailer’s shares have halved. An Australian billionaire who amassed a 13% stake has sparked defensive tactics, but it’s an opportunity for boss Hillary Super to initiate a search for a buyer keen to remake the company in private.

The 48-year-old chain, which gained fame by featuring supermodels in its catalogs and TV ads, had been housed within L Brands. A series of missteps attracted the attention of pushy investor Barington Capital, which successfully urged a separation from soap seller Bath & Body Works (BBWI). Neither company has benefited from the split.

Efforts to create a leaner Victoria’s Secret worked, but they have failed to impress investors. It achieved a three-year cost-cutting goal of more than $250 million ahead of schedule. And yet its $7.4 billion sales goal for 2026 is considered too ambitious, based on the $6.3 billion penciled in by analysts, according to Visible Alpha. A cybersecurity breach disclosed on Tuesday, which shut down corporate systems last month and is expected to hit future quarterly results, won’t help either.

Super, who was named CEO last August, is trying to recharge growth, including by bringing back sports-bra brand VSX. It will be difficult to recapture the former glory at Victoria’s Secret, however. Its market share in U.S. women’s underwear tumbled to 18% last year from almost 27% in 2019, per Euromonitor data.

At this stage, it would be better to lean into the outside interest. Victoria’s Secret adopted a poison pill, or the ability to flood the market with shares, in response to the unwanted stake accumulation by Brett Blundy’s Sydney-based investment shop BBRC, accusing the buyer of trying to gain control without paying a premium.

Victoria’s Secret should interest others, too. If a buyout firm paid a 30% premium, it would value the equity at about $2.4 billion. With less than $600 million of net debt and roughly the same amount of EBITDA, there’s also room to borrow to fund an acquisition.

Assume a new owner could lift sales by 2% a year and the operating margin by 1 percentage point, to 10%. At the same 5 times EBITDA valuation in five years, Victoria’s Secret would generate a 24% return, according to Breakingviews calculations. Like lacy undergarments, a company is sometimes better appreciated only after it has been kept under wraps.

Context News

  • Victoria’s Secret said on June 3 that it had temporarily shut down its website and corporate systems on May 26 after detecting a cybersecurity incident. The website was restored on May 29.
  • The lingerie retailer added that it would postpone the release of complete financial results for the first quarter ended May 3, originally slated for June 5. It said it is expecting net sales of $1.4 billion and $32 million in adjusted operating income, and that the breach will affect future results.
  • Victoria’s Secret adopted a shareholder rights plan on May 20 after Australian billionaire Brett Blundy’s investment firm increased its stake. As of April, BBRC holds about 10.3 million shares, or about 13% of the lingerie maker.
  • The so-called poison pill was put in place to “guard against tactics to gain control of the company without paying all shareholders an appropriate premium for that control,” Victoria’s Secret Chair Donna James said.
  • Under the plan, the company said it would issue one right per share on May 29, which would become active if a shareholder acquired at least a 15% stake.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Mortgage Loans Are The Key To Growth For Northpointe Bancshares
data.description:
On February 14 of this year, Northpointe Bancshares, Inc. (NYSE:NPB) began trading on the NYSE. The company’s debut fell short of its initial plans, as the IPO price of $14.50 was below the projected range of $16 to $18 per share. The total offering of 10.4 million shares raised $151 million for the sole owner and operator of Northpointe Bank.

NPB started the month of June almost 10% below its IPO price. However, at first glance, several of the company’s basic financial metrics seemed attractive to me as a relatively conservative investor. I decided to take a deeper look to get the full story on this new stock.

While much of the available financial information for NPB has a relatively short history, there is a lot to like about the company and its current position in the market as a mortgage lender. However, there is enough uncertainty for both the company itself and the current state of the mortgage industry to keep me on the sidelines for now. I am starting my coverage of Northpointe Bancshares with a hold rating for the reasons discussed below.

Company Snapshot

Founded in 1999, Northpointe Bancshares currently serves clients in all 50 states under the name of its subsidiary, Northpointe Bank. The company has only one physical bank branch location in Grand Rapids, Michigan, but it also controls loan production offices in 15 different states. According to its website, Northpointe Bank offers “a nationwide mortgage purchase program, residential mortgage loans, digital deposit banking to our retail customers, and custodial deposit services to our loan servicing clients.”

In its first investor presentation made in February of this year, Northpointe Bank released an overview of its four business segments with financial data updated through the end of Q3 2024. The highlights for each are given in the snapshot below. All totaled, at the end of Q4 2024, NPB had $5.2 billion in assets, while controlling $3.8 billion in total deposits. Total loans for the bank were valued at $4.4 billion.

The stock currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.025, giving NPB a dividend yield of 0.76%. While the sample size is limited, the current yield for the stock is below both its peers as well as the median payout for other companies in its sector. The company did note in its investor presentation that greater dividends were paid to shareholders before NPB’s IPO. Annual dividends totaled $0.45 in 2021 and $0.30 in 2022.

Analysis of Northpointe Bancshares

Northpointe Bancshares released its first quarterly report as a publicly traded company on April 23. Earnings per share were $0.49, topping estimates by $0.03 and beating the Q4 2024 number by $0.15. Revenues for the bank in the first three months of this year were $53.3 million, just over $4.3 million better than expectations. The company is expected to make $2.22 in FY 2025 and $2.74 in FY 2026. If that happens, the company will trade at a PE under 5 at the end of 2026 with earnings growth of 23.4%.

There was more good news in NPB’s first 10-Q of 2025. Total assets grew 12% from the end of 2024. Total deposits grew 11.7% while the bank’s loan portfolio increased by 16.3%. The key driver of this growth is the company’s mortgage purchase program, or MPP. This program allows NPBs to purchase residential mortgages from other lenders. The bank can then hold these loans on its balance sheet or sell them in the secondary market.

In Q1 2025, the company purchased almost 16,000 loans at a total value of $6.7 billion. Loans are generally held for 30 days or less, and the total sales volume from this quarter was $6.3 billion. The loan yield from these activities was 7.14%. In its IPO prospectus, the bank claims to have proprietary technology that enables it to facilitate these transactions quickly and efficiently. One rather impressive claim from NPB’s initial investor presentation is that the bank has never charged off any of its loans from its mortgage purchase program in the last 15 years. Then again, if the large majority of the loans are held for only 30 days, then that statistic seems somewhat meaningless.

The rapid growth currently being experienced by the company is good, until it isn’t. By that, I mean that the bank’s reliance on the MPP program exposes it to greater risk from interest rates and deteriorating consumer credit. The company noted that its provisions for credit losses have been rapidly increasing due to “credit migration trends” and “changes in the economic forecasts” that it is using for the remainder of the year. In Q1 2025, the company made a provision for credit losses of $1.3 million. This was a change from the same period in 2024 when they claimed a benefit of $357,000.

The bank has a low share of non-interest bearing deposits, 6.1%. While Northpointe Bank is not a traditional bank in many ways, because of its greater focus on buying and selling mortgage loans. Nonetheless, its cost for keeping its growing deposit base is well ahead of what would be expected. NPB’s counter to its relatively high costs of deposits is the growth of its “All-In-One” home financing product that combines a HELOC with a deposit account. As can be seen in the chart below, these loans now make up over 12% of the company’s total debt portfolio.

The Current State of the US Mortgage Industry

Household debt in the US is currently at an all-time high. At the end of Q1 2025, the total due to lenders equaled $18.2 trillion, a 28.7% increase since the start of 2020. Mortgage loans make up 70% of that total, 72% if home equity loans are added in.

In May of this year, the New York Fed reported that about 62,000 homeowners entered foreclosure territory on their home loans in Q1 2025. That was an increase of over 50% from Q4 2024 and a 38.6% increase from the first quarter of last year. Transition rates for home loans moving into the category of "90+ days late" are approaching levels not seen since before the pandemic, and there appears to be a steady stream of loans entering the 30–60 days late category that could soon push that 90+ days number even higher. While we are still way short of where things were during the housing crisis, the trend is working in the wrong direction for companies that make their living off the dependability of mortgage payments.

According to the National Association of Realtors, existing home sales in the US in April fell 0.5% from March’s total and a full 2.0% from April 2024. The supply of homes is increasing while a number of buyers are staying on the sidelines. Persistently higher interest rates have thrown a wet blanket over a market that was red-hot just a couple of years ago. Should greater supply eventually cause home prices to decline, it could present another problem for NPB if defaults are also rising.

On top of all of this, the mortgage origination industry is becoming more and more competitive. Rocket Companies, Inc. (RKT) recently acquired both Mr. Cooper Group Inc. (COOP) and Redfin Corporation (RDFN). Investors who want more information about the potential impact of those transactions would be well served to read Julia Ostian’s recent article on Seeking Alpha about RKT. While NPB is in a better position on its balance sheet than RKT because of lower debt, the article points out the fact that the US housing market is shrinking and the competition for mortgage servicing is becoming more intense.

Risks to Consider

From my perspective, the biggest risk associated with an investment in NPB is its heavy reliance on mortgage lending. The company’s main source of revenue is heavily dependent on interest rates, the US housing market, and the strength of the overall economy. If rates were to rise quickly, demand for new mortgages would likely become depressed. If rates were to experience a rapid decline, the value of existing assets could decrease.

Northpointe Bank is attempting to grow a larger share of a highly competitive market. Margins could easily come under pressure as yields fall, which would impact net interest income. Any high-profile bank failures could also undermine consumer confidence, increase the cost of deposit insurance, and bring on increased government regulation.

The Bottom Line

There are two main reasons that I am hesitant to issue a buy rating on NPB shares at this time. The first is simply the stock’s short history and somewhat limited track record. There is also a lock-up period of 180 days for executive officers, directors, and shareholders who own more than 2% of the company. That will expire on August 13 of this year, and it is reasonable to assume there will be some added selling pressure on the stock.

The second is that the company seems to have most of its eggs in the home mortgage business at a time when consumer debt is climbing and the housing market is on shaky ground. I am rating the stock as a hold at this time, with plans to revisit in the final quarter of this year once the lockup period has passed.

data.title: Strong Jobs Data Signals Higher Rates Ahead
data.description:
The jobs report came in better than expected, rising by 139,000 in May, exceeding the estimate of 130,000, while the unemployment rate remained at 4.2%. Additionally, wage growth was significantly better than expected, rising by 0.4% month-over-month (m/m) versus estimates of 0.3%, while climbing by 3.9% year-over-year (y/y) versus estimates of 3.8%.

The job report means that the interest rate on the back of the yield curve continues to rise in the form of a bear steepener. The 10-year rate (US10Y) is climbing by around 10 bps on the day to 4.49%. Still, the rate should be much higher than that, given that the Fed Funds rate is unlikely to be cut significantly from its current level, unless a recession hits the U.S., which, at present, could be a long way off, based on an index within the employment report.

For 2025, the Fed fund futures are pricing in a rate of 3.92%, which means the Fed would cut rates less than 2 times in 2025. The December 2026 terminal rate is projected to decrease to 3.17%, indicating approximately 100 basis points of rate cuts between now and the end of 2026. Overall, as long as recession is avoided, it seems clear that the market believes the Fed doesn't have much room to cut rates from the current level.

Higher Long-End Rates

It goes back to the argument from over the weekend, that the yield curve today is too flat, and that interest rates at the back of the yield curve still have plenty of room to rise from current levels, with the 10-year rate trading just 15 bps above the current effective Fed funds rate. Historically, the spread peaks at much higher levels.

Therefore, unless the Fed Funds rate falls to 2%, which is more than 100 basis points less than current Fed Funds Future pricing, the 10-year rate is likely to increase by at least 50 basis points from current levels. Assuming a 3% Fed Funds rate and spreads of 200 basis points, the 10-year rate should trade at a minimum of around 5%, and could peak with a spread of around 300 basis points at 6%.

Even the term premium itself seems too low, given that the years of easy monetary policy are behind us, while quantitative easing ended long ago. It would seem that, given that monetary policy is now at higher levels, the likelihood of a quantitative easing returning anytime soon may be far off. This suggests that term premiums themselves should be higher.

Growth Remains Solid

Additionally, the job report itself reveals that growth in the overall economy is likely still running around a 4 to 5% nominal growth rate. This is given that the index of aggregate weekly payrolls continues to rise steadily at a rate of around 4.5 to 5% year-over-year, going back to the beginning of 2024. That index tends to track closely with year-over-year changes in nominal GDP growth.

Certainly, recession risk is always a genuine concern, and it can dramatically alter the outlook, but currently, most indicators and data suggest that a recession is not imminent. The most viable option for mitigating economic risk would remain in a stagflation-like environment, where we have slowing economic growth and elevated inflation rates, which some data suggests poses a threat. But even in that case, a valid argument can be made that rates on the back of the yield curve rise.

Given this, the risk to long-term yields is skewed to the upside. Unless economic growth markedly deteriorates or inflation sharply declines—both of which seem unlikely based on current indicators—the trajectory for 10-year and 30-year rates remains higher, and further yield curve steepening is likely.

data.title: MSA Safety Alters Strategies To Negate The Near-Term Challenges
data.description:
MSA To Remain Soft

I last discussed MSA Safety Incorporated (NYSE:MSA) on July 16, 2024, and you can read the latest article here. In recent times, MSA’s order rate stabilized as the sales growth of detection and industrial PPE products picked up. So, the company continues to roll out new protection gear, jackets, and helmets. It has implemented a price hike that can help improve its operating margin over time. The recent acquisition will also strengthen its detection capabilities portfolio.

However, the uncertainty over the tariff structure can impact its costs in the short term. The US manufacturing environment, too, is unconducive to expansion. Nonetheless, the cash flow improvement allowed it to increase dividends. The stock remains relatively overvalued. Considering the short-term challenges and the company’s actions to mitigate their impact, I maintain my “hold” call.

Why Do I Keep My Rating Unchanged?

In my previous article published in July 2024, I rated MSA a “Hold.” I wrote:

MSA’s primary focus remains on realizing long-term growth and balance sheet efficiency. I think the company’s actions to increase shareholder returns through share repurchase following positive free cash flow generation will boost investors’ sentiment. Its relative valuation appears to be at a premium. Despite that, I think the stock deserves a "hold" call given the strategic initiatives.

In Q1, strong demand for detection products and the industrial PPE category offset lower Fire Service sales. While sales stabilized, the company’s EBITDA margin showed signs of weakness. Its cash flow improved, leading to a dividend hike. However, the stock remains relatively overvalued. I continue to rate it a "hold."

Orders and New Products

MSA’s order rate was steady in Q1 and remained stable at the start of Q2, in line with its historical trend. Its Q1 orders included a $10 million breathing apparatus contract in Southern California. The order is to be delivered in 2025. The company continues to entrench its presence in LA City and LA County through successive orders in these regions.

MSA expects above-market growth in fire detection and fixed and portable devices. It is bullish on portable gas detection devices, including the io4 gas monitor. Recently, it has introduced the G1 SCBA XR Edition and the new Globe G-XTREME PRO turnout gear jacket to improve firefighter protection, comfort, and operational awareness. In late 2024, it launched the new Cairns 1836 Fire Helmet.

Tariffs and Supply Chain Impact

MSA has a global supply chain. So, the recent tariff hike can impact its cost structure. Currently, approximately 15% of its cost of sales is subject to tariffs, with one-third of these coming from China. It has laid a long-term strategic path. It has implemented targeted price increases in April. The full impact of the price hike may take months to be fully realized.

MSA is also improving productivity through sourcing initiatives, supply chain, and manufacturing initiatives. I think the tariff hike will have an adverse effect on its costs in the short term, but the company’s proactive approach can nullify the impact.

Recent Acquisition

In May 2025, MSA acquired M&C TechGroup. The acquisition expanded MSA's Detection solutions and added new capabilities in the gas analysis and process safety business. M&C TechGroup has an annual turnover of $55 million and is expected to be accretive to MSA’s FY2025 earnings.

2025 Outlook

In 2025, MSA will see a trend of stable order as it diversifies across products, geographies, and markets. Expects FY2025 revenues to increase by “low-single digit”. However, the macro uncertainty and the global tariff activity can mar the growth. In Q1, strong demand for detection products and customers’ acceleration of certain shipments in consideration of tariffs disproportionately benefited revenues. So, in the following few quarters, sales can underperform the growth achieved in Q1.

Industry Indicators And My Estimates

The US ISM Manufacturing PMI contracted to 48.7 in May, marking the third consecutive month of contraction in the manufacturing sector. The current economic uncertainty, sustained cost pressures, lower output, new orders, and employment culminated in the successively falling PMI. An index below 50 points to activity contraction and can hurt MSA’s outlook.

Over the past 17 quarters, its adjusted EBITDA increased by 6% on average. Given the cross currents of tariff hikes and the company’s mitigation initiatives, I expect the EBITDA growth to stabilize at the current level. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 6% to 8%.

The Q1 Drivers

As disclosed in the Q1 press release dated April 29, MSA’s year-over-year revenues in the Americas declined by 1%. Much of the growth was related to Detection and Industrial PPE, offset by lower Fire Service sales. The segment’s operating income margin contracted by 240 basis points due to adverse currency movements in Latin America and inflation.

In contrast, its international business segment sales increased by 9% in Q1, due primarily to strong growth in Detection and Fire Service. The adjusted operating margin expanded by 310 basis points due to a favorable sales mix and SG&A leverage.

Cash Flows And Debt Level

In Q1 2025, MSA's cash flow from operations increased by 22% compared to a year ago. Although revenues remained nearly unchanged, lower cash used for variable compensation contributed to the cash flow rise. Free cash flow (or FCF) increased by 29% in Q1.

MSA’s debt-to-equity ratio was 0.54x as of March 31, 2025. The leverage ratio was also higher than its competitors (GRC, BRC, TNC) average leverage of 0.45x. Its liquidity (cash & equivalents plus revolving credit facility) totaled $1.07 billion as of that date. In May, the company raised the quarterly dividend from $0.51 to $0.53 per share, or by 4%, reflecting the company’s robust free cash flow generation.

Relative Valuation and Target Price

MSA's forward EV/EBITDA multiple expansion versus the current EV/EBITDA contrasts with its peers, which implies its EBITDA is expected to decline versus a rise in EBITDA for its peers in the next four quarters. This typically reflects in a much lower EV/EBITDA multiple compared to its peers. However, its EV/EBITDA multiple (13.9x) is higher than its peers' (GRC, BRC, and TNC) average of 11.4x. So, the stock is relatively overvalued. It is trading below its past five-year average.

MSA's five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple is 17.9x. If the stock trades at this multiple, it can increase by 30%, as I described in my previous article. If it trades at its peers' average, the stock price can decline by 19%. Since my last publication in July 2024, where I suggested a "hold," the stock has decreased by approximately 17%.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect 6%-8% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and assuming the current EV/EBITDA multiple of 13.9x holds, I think the stock should trade between $172.5 and $176, implying a 7% upside.

Wall Street Rating

Three sell-side analysts have rated MSA a "strong buy." Two analysts rated it a "hold," while none rated it a "sell." The consensus target price is $177.2, suggesting nearly a 9% upside at the current price. Considering the current drivers, I think the Wall Street analysts are realistic in their estimates.

What's The Take On MSA?

In 2025 so far, the US industrial and manufacturing environment has remained under pressure. On top of that, the US tariff policy and related uncertainty appear to have dented MSA’s near-term outlook. So, the stock underperformed the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) in the past year.

However, MSA’s management has faced economic cycles in the past and put in place a strategy to encounter them. Its proactive steps in strengthening the supply chains can pay dividends in mitigating the impact of the tariff raises. Its recent acquisition can also improve earnings. The recent dividend hike following positive free cash flow generation will boost investors’ sentiment. Although its relative valuation appears to be at a premium, the stock deserves a continued "hold" call in my opinion.

data.title: Xtract One Technologies Inc. 2025 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Xtract One Technologies Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: The Labor Market Remains Too Strong For Fed Easing
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this morning, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported the employment data for the economy during the month of May. In May, the US economy created 139,000 jobs, which was slightly higher than expected. Over the last twelve months, the economy created 1.7 million jobs, which is consistent with the soft landing of the labor market. The unemployment rate remained at 4.2%. While the US labor market continues to remain quiet, there is no data on the employment side of the Federal Reserve’s dual mandate to suggest that monetary policy needs to move in any direction.

Leading Indicators Prior to Report

Like the market, I was surprised to see a stronger job creation number this morning as prior to this morning’s report, new data presented this week showed the labor market was still cool, and the picture was mixed. The Bureau of Labor Statistics released the April Job Openings and Labor Survey (JOLTS) showing increased activity in the labor market. Hires and separations both rose in the month of April, but continue to remain below their pre-pandemic numbers, signaling that flows in the labor market (hiring, firing, quitting) are continuing at a slow pace.

Job openings rose in the month of April, bucking a mostly downward trend over the past couple of years. When compared to unemployment data, the economy continues to have more job openings than unemployed people, although the number is very close to parity, versus a shortage of over 5 million people in the labor force during the summer of 2022.

In terms of data during the month of May, weekly jobless claims pointed to a slowing picture in the labor market. First-time jobless claims rose above 245,000 last week, which is one of the highest levels in the past year. The four-week moving average for initial claims has steadily risen from under 225,000 to over 235,000 in less than two months.

On a continuing basis, ongoing jobless claims, which measure the number of workers still unemployed, rose to above 1.9 million during the month of May, which is at their highest level in at least a year. The four-week moving average has charged up by nearly 40,000 people over the last two months. While the jobless claims data doesn’t flash recession, it certainly hints at a growing base of unemployed people.

Several Indicators in the May Jobs Report Show Strength in the Labor Market

There are several items that investors should feel good about with respect to the strength of the labor market in the May employment report. First, the unemployment rate remains 0.2% below the Federal Reserve’s estimate for 2025. Additionally, the Fed’s long-term or projected level of unemployment at full employment is 4.2%, right where the current indicator is, suggesting the economy is at full employment.

Along with the regular unemployment measure, the U6 rate, which includes part-time workers seeking full-time work, remained at 7.8% for the month of May. While the U6 has slowly edged higher, it matches with the normalization of the labor market as the U6U3 spread (which measures the additional slack in the labor force) appears to be holding at late 2024 levels.

Trends in unemployment also saw improvement in the average duration of unemployment, which fell by nearly 1.5 weeks to 21.8 weeks in the month of May. Additionally, the number of people unemployed for more than 27 weeks dropped by more than 200,000 to 1.46 million. These declines are coming off concerning elevations, so I would classify the improvement as normalization versus strength.

What to Watch For – Changes in Labor Force Size

One of the reasons we’ve seen the unemployment rate stay below expectations has been the number of people leaving the labor force. In May alone, 800,000 adults left the labor force, as the total number of adults not in the labor force approached 103 million. In the last 12 months, nearly 2.5 million adults have left the labor force. When people leave the labor force (usually via retirement), it pushes the economy to hire replacements and shrinks the number of unemployed people. Should many of these individuals re-enter the labor force for any reason, the number of unemployed people will rise, and with it the unemployment rate.

Conclusion

The labor market continues to operate at a level consistent with full employment, with the unemployment rate remaining slightly lower than the Fed’s 2025 target. The Federal Reserve meets in less than two weeks to decide interest rates and issue new economic projections. I don’t believe the labor market can influence any change in monetary policy, and I believe the Federal Reserve’s economic projections regarding the labor market will remain the same.

data.title: May CPI Preview: Can You Even Trust The Data?
data.description:
The consensus

The U.S. Bureau of Labor is set to release the CPI inflation data for May on Wednesday, June 11th. The analyst consensus expectations are that:

  • The core CPI inflation will increase by 0.3% MoM, which is above the monthly inflation rate in April at 0.2%, and due to the base effect, the annual Core CPI is expected to increase from 2.8% to 3%.
  • The headline CPI inflation will increase also by 0.2% MoM, at the same rate as in April, which would keep the annual headline CPI inflation unchanged at 2.3%.

Thus, the expectations are that the core CPI inflation will remain well above the Fed's 2% target, while the headline inflation is expected to be near the 2% target. Note, that the Fed is more focused on the core CPI inflation, which means that inflation is still a problem.

In fact, due to the base effects, the core CPI inflation is expected to spike over the next 3 months to 3.3-3.5% if the monthly inflation readings remain at 0.3%.

Specifically, as the chart below shows, the inflation readings over the next three months will replace the very low monthly core inflation readings from the same period in 2024.

Inflation likely to surprise to the upside

However, it is very likely that the actual inflation readings over the next few months will be higher than expected, specifically due to the tariffs in place.

Specifically, these are the current tariffs in place, that are expected to boost inflation:

  • The universal 10% tariff on all imports - this should affect the prices of all imported goods
  • The 30% tariff on all imports from China - expected to affect the prices of all goods imported from China
  • The additional 25% tariff on autos, which is expected to affect the prices of imported cars, domestically produced cars with foreign parts, but also used cars
  • First 25%, and then a 50% tariff on steel and aluminum, which is expected to affect the prices of all goods using these inputs in production.

Thus, it is likely that inflation figures could be higher than expected. If the core CPI comes at 0.4%, the annual inflation is likely to spike above 4% by the end of 2025.

Note, that companies are already stating that there will be a 50% pass-through to consumers within three months. Unlike in 2017, this time companies are expected to absorb around 50% of tariffs and pass the other 50% to consumers via higher prices. Given that the labor market is still solid, it is likely that the consumer will allow for this increase without affecting the demand, which almost guarantees higher inflation.

Can you trust the data?

However, according to the Wall Street Journal, economists are increasingly concerned that the CPI data is not accurate.

Specifically, it seems like due to the cuts in spending, the government agency that reports the CPI inflation data BLS is short of staff needed to accurately collect and process the inflation data.

Thus, the quality of CPI inflation data is questionable. The CPI data collection is done by so-called "enumerators," who basically go to stores, offices, businesses, and others to collect the prices of goods and services—in different geographical regions. This data is then aggregated and reported as the CPI inflation, by category.

Now, due to the cuts, there are fewer enumerators, and as a result, some geographical areas are not covered, and some products are not directly measured. Thus, the new data aggregation uses increasingly a price guessing method called “different-cell imputation.”

This means that the reported CPI data will be more or less a guess of what the true CPI inflation is—and that's a major problem.

Now, economists don't think that this is an attempt to manipulate the data. However, there is no way of knowing whether the reported data is influenced, given that it is more or less a guess.

Thus, the reported actual data could actually be lower than expected if it's influenced. So, the problem is whether the CPI data be trusted for the purpose of investing and also policymaking.

Calm before storm

It's important to note that inflation is now largely at the Fed's target, based on the quarterly annualized inflation readings. The headline CPI is actually at 1.7%, while the core CPI is at 2.36%.

Thus, the Fed should be thinking about cutting interest rates at this point, and these should be "the goods cuts."

However, these inflation figures don't reflect the currently imposed tariffs in place, as previously discussed. Even more importantly, tariffs are expected to increase, as more sector-based tariffs will be likely imposed (pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, airline parts...), and also some reciprocal tariffs are likely to be imposed after the 90-day delay expires in July and August for China.

As a result, we are currently calm before the tariff-based induced inflationary storm. Inflation is likely to spike over the next 12 months.

Implications

Inflation is expected to spike over the next few months due to the tariffs in place, and over the next year if additional tariffs are imposed.

Thus, obviously, this is a negative for the Bond market, as long-term rates (TLT) are expected to increase, with the 10-Year Treasury yields (US10Y) rising above 5%—especially if Trump's tax cuts blow up the deficits.

In addition, the Fed will not be able to cut interest rates, even if the economy slows, which is also expected. Specifically, the net effect of the Trump policies (immigration, DOGE, and tariffs) is anti-growth. Thus, the economy is likely to slip into stagflation.

Stagflation is a worst-case scenario for the stock market—the economy is likely to slow, and possibly contract, while high inflation will prevent the Fed from cutting interest rates. Given that the S&P 500 (SP500) is still trading at bubble-like valuations, with the P/E ratio at 24, my outlook is highly negative—the bubble is facing stagflation.

However, the recent bounce in the stock market continues, and it's led again by the AI theme and the mega-cap tech stocks, like Nvidia (NVDA). The bubble could continue to inflate as long as the hard data does not confirm the stagflation.

So, now we are back at a discussion about the trust in the quality of the reported data. If the reported CPI cannot be trusted, and the reported data is lower than it should be, the stock market and the bond market could be distorted. If that's the case, and the Fed is pressured into cutting interest rates by the "low-quality data," these distortions could be magnified. We could be flying blind here.

data.title: Peloton Interactive: Sell Rated As The Business Is Likely To Stay Weak In The Near-Term
data.description:
Investment Overview

I give a sell rating for Peloton Interactive (NASDAQ:PTON) as I believe the business is going to continue seeing negative revenue growth. Hardware sales are collapsing, subscriber losses are accelerating, and the macro environment remains unsupportive. Execution weakness and unresolved operational inefficiencies are making things worse, which makes a meaningful recovery unlikely in the near term.

Business Description

PTON is branded as a connected fitness company, where they incorporate hardware, software, and content to create a premium at-home workout experience. Viewed simply, PTON's primary sales model is to sell the hardware (e.g., the Bike), followed by upselling users to subscribe to it. Through a monthly membership, users gain access to a library of live and on-demand classes across cycling, running, strength, yoga, and more. The hardware business is the lower-margin one, and the recurring subscriptions revenue stream is the high-margin profit driver.

Macroeconomic Pressures Demand Outlook

I believe the macro backdrop remains a major overhang for PTON. While fitness spending may be resilient in general, the appetite for such spending is not universal. PTON’s high-priced hardware still falls into the discretionary category, and it is not a category that does well in a weak macro environment. Management itself has flagged softness in sales in late 3Q25 and early April, which to me says that underlying demand is poor and has not seen any meaningful signs of recovery. PTON's operating metrics evidently show that it is hurt by this macro environment. Revenue continues to decline—down 2/9/13% in 1/2/3Q25. But the real red flag is that connected fitness product revenue fell 27% y/y in 3Q25, which is a steep and troubling drop. Subscriber losses are also piling up, with 3Q25 marking the fifth straight quarter of contraction and the sharpest decline yet at -6% y/y.

As I mentioned above, the hardware is the main point of “entry” for PTON to convert consumers into members, which brings in high-margin recurring revenue. When hardware sales collapse, that funnel dries up. Also, the fact that PTON needs to push for refurbished unit sales suggests demand for new products just isn’t there anymore. The weakness in selling hardware and converting buyers to subscribers also has long-term implications on growth, as it means PTON has a smaller base of customers to monetize.

For PTON to see a positive inflection here, the macro conditions have to turn for the better, and I don’t think that is going to materialize anytime soon.

App Strategy Is Not Working

On top of the above, it also does not help to know that PTON’s app strategy is not working as well as it should. For info, paid Connect Fitness subscribers are users who own a Peloton-connected fitness product. Paid app subscribers are those who do not own a connected fitness product but access it through the PTON app. The idea is this: it can significantly reduce the adoption hurdle for PTON content. It works well on paper, but there are signs that this strategy is simply not working.

Firstly, the downward revision to FY25 paid app subscription guidance (now reflecting a 30,000 drop at the midpoint) is the most obvious red flag. The reason provided was due to limiting media spend and investment in attracting new corporate wellness clients. But in my view, if subscriber strength is dependent on “media” spend”—which I take as marketing—then it speaks very poorly on the organic demand for this product. This has huge implications. If a low-friction, low-cost product like this struggles to attract and retain users without aggressive marketing, it just shows that the app is not appealing to users. Then it raises the question of how strong the entire PTON subscription ecosystem is.

This draws back to the sustainability of PTON’s entire growth engine. If the app is not appealing, then consumers are really just buying expensive hardware, which is just not an attractive business (there are a lot of competitors selling the same hardware product). The pullback in the Corporate Wellness channel (note this was previously positioned as a key growth lever) also suggests the initiative failed to gain traction.

Taken together, these moves paint a picture of a brand that is struggling to broaden its reach.

Operational Inefficiencies and Tech Debt Create a Drag on Profitability

PTON is not only facing external issues, but there are also a lot of issues internally. The biggest concern I have is that PTON appears to be operating in a very inefficient manner. For instance, PTON needed to shut down warehouses for three days each quarter just to conduct manual inventory counts. This simply doesn’t make any sense given that PTON is a business that is worth $2.8 billion. I would’ve expected PTON to be using a top-notch inventory tracking system to facilitate the business. The current way of doing things may have worked when PTON was small and scaling, but when it is this big ($2.5 billion in sales), it shows up in the P&L and is a headwind to margins. I see this as a “tech debt.”

Aside from these, other “tech debts” include the lack of proper diagnostic tools. Support agents often have to send technicians for multiple in-home visits, and this not only frustrates members, but also drives up the entire service cost as more labor hours are needed. These are not issues that management is not aware of. They know these are a source of churn, but nothing has really changed despite them knowing it.

Put together, these inefficiencies are major hurdles to execution, and any meaningful fix will likely require heavy investment—something that could weigh on margins.

Valuation

When I compare PTON to its peers in the broader fitness and equipment industry, I can see why this is a stock that will continue to trade at a discount. Here are some peers for comparison. Garmin has an expected revenue growth outlook of high single digits and trades at 18x forward EBITDA. Technogym Spa has a low-teens revenue growth CAGR outlook and trades at 11x forward EBITDA (a discount to GRMN because it has a much lower adj. EBITDA margin of ~19% vs. GRMN at 29%). Logically, a company with lower growth and margin than these numbers should trade at a discount, and indeed, PTON is a business I expect to see negative revenue growth for in the near term, with that view in mind, I also don’t think PTON will flip into generating meaningful profits anytime soon. Consensus numbers reflect this. They expect PTON to see negative revenue growth and continue to stay in negative EBITDA territory. Hence, I believe PTON, which trades at ~9.5x today, is at a justified discount to peers.

Risk

Internally, if management can make good progress in streamlining operations, it could be a real boost to margins. PTON is also considering raising subscription prices for the first time in over three years, and while I believe this will elevate churn, if it doesn’t, it could provide a meaningful uplift to margins. Also, while the connected fitness product segment is currently seeing demand headwinds, any signs of broader macro recovery could reignite demand.

Conclusion

I give a sell rating for PTON. Not only is the macro situation bad for PTON, but also its brand appears to have fading consumer relevance, the business operates inefficiently, and I do not see a clear fix to all these. Both hardware and software demand are deteriorating, and internal “tech debt” will likely require material investment to resolve.
